votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, including the default '0' padding for out-of-bounds indices in string b.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, including the default '0' padding for out-of-bounds characters in string b.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, including the default '0' padding for string b when it is shorter than string a.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. char_to_bool, bool_to_char, and xor_ascii match their Coq counterparts. string_xor_spec checks that len(result) == len(a) and that for each index i < len(a), result[i] equals xor_ascii(a[i], b[i]) where b[i] defaults to '0' if i >= len(b). This matches the Coq spec which uses nth with default '0' for out-of-bounds access in lb.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. char_to_bool, bool_to_char, and xor_ascii match their Coq counterparts. string_xor_spec checks that result has the same length as a, and for each index i < len(a), it verifies that result[i] equals xor_ascii(a[i], b[i]) where b[i] defaults to '0' if i >= len(b), matching the Coq nth function's default behavior. The Python returns True iff the Coq Prop would hold.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. char_to_bool, bool_to_char, and xor_ascii match their Coq counterparts. string_xor_spec checks that result has the same length as a, and for each index i < len(a), verifies that result[i] equals xor_ascii(a[i], b[i]) where b[i] defaults to '0' if i >= len(b), matching the Coq nth default behavior. The Python returns True iff the Coq Prop would hold for the same concrete inputs.
Matches Coq: enforces result length equals a’s length and per-index XOR of chars where b[i] defaults to '0'; char_to_bool and xor behavior align with Coq definitions.
Both require result length equals a’s length and check each position is XOR of a[i] and b[i] (using '0' when b is short), with only '1' mapping to true and all other characters to false.
Python mirrors the Coq spec: maps only '1' to true, XORs via booleans, requires result length equals a, and pads b with '0' when shorter; per-index comparison matches the Coq nth-based definition.
